topic: The political fight dominating July 13-14, 2026 is President Trump's declaration that the United States will reinstate a naval blockade of Iran and charge a 20% fee on all cargo transiting the Strait of Hormuz — branding America 'the Guardian of the Hormuz Strait' — and the argument over whether collecting a toll to keep the world's most important oil chokepoint open is legitimate burden-sharing or an illegal 'protection racket' that violates the very freedom-of-navigation principle the U.S. invokes against Iran. On Monday, July 13, Trump announced on Truth Social that the U.S. was 'reinstating THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE' and would be 'reimbursed, at the rate of 20% on all cargo shipped, for any and all costs necessary to do the job of providing safety and security' in the strait, insisting 'The Hormuz Strait is OPEN, and will remain OPEN, with or without Iran.' The move followed the collapse of a June 14 U.S.-Iran ceasefire that had provided toll-free Hormuz passage and barred Iran from charging fees; Trump declared the truce 'over' on July 7 after Iran attacked commercial vessels, and the U.S. resumed strikes (Central Command said the operations would 'degrade their ability to attack innocent civilians and commercial shipping'). The checkable core: what Trump announced and when; what international law (the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea and the International Maritime Organization) says about charging mandatory tolls to transit an international strait; the fact that Secretary of State Marco Rubio had days earlier said no nation should have to pay to transit the strait; how much of the world's oil (~20%, ~20 million barrels per day, per the EIA) passes through Hormuz; Iran's disputed claim to have closed the strait and charged tolls up to $2 million per vessel; the War Powers Resolution posture (Trump notified Congress July 10 that strikes began July 7, and Democrats dispute the 60-day clock could reset); and whether this fits a broader transactional pattern of reframing U.S. security guarantees as fees the U.S. collects.
question: On July 13, 2026, President Trump announced that the United States would reinstate a naval blockade of Iran and charge a 20% fee on all cargo passing through the Strait of Hormuz, declaring America 'THE GUARDIAN OF THE HORMUZ STRAIT' and insisting the waterway 'is OPEN, and will remain OPEN, with or without Iran.' The strait carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil (about 20 million barrels per day, per the U.S. Energy Information Administration). The announcement came after a June 14 U.S.-Iran ceasefire — which had provided toll-free Hormuz passage and prohibited Iran from imposing charges — collapsed; Trump declared the truce 'over' on July 7 after Iranian attacks on commercial ships, and U.S. forces resumed strikes. Defenders frame the fee as fair reimbursement for the cost of keeping a vital global chokepoint open and safe, a form of burden-sharing, and note that the U.S. has kept Hormuz open by force before and that ~20% of world oil is at stake. Critics — and the United Nations' International Maritime Organization — say there is 'no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait,' that the fee violates the freedom-of-navigation principle codified in the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (which bars charging ships for mere passage), that it is a 'protection racket' mirroring the exact Iranian tolls the U.S. condemned days earlier (Secretary of State Rubio had said 'there isn't a nation on Earth that supports having to pay money to go through the straits'), and that the renewed blockade and strikes amount to an unauthorized war that Trump cannot restart under the War Powers Resolution. The checkable core: the facts and timing of the announcement; what UNCLOS and the IMO say about strait tolls; the contradiction with the administration's own prior position; the oil-volume stakes; Iran's disputed closure/toll claims; the War Powers posture; and how this fits a transactional 'make them pay for U.S. protection' pattern.
summary:
The most-argued political story of July 13-14, 2026 was President Trump's declaration that the U.S. will reinstate a blockade of Iran and collect a 20% fee on all cargo transiting the Strait of Hormuz, branding America 'the Guardian of the Hormuz Strait.' This is a genuinely two-sided day that leans slightly toward the critics — not because the underlying concern (keeping the strait open) is wrong, and not because the president lacks any authority to act, but because the specific move at the center of the argument, a toll to transit an international strait, has no basis in international law and contradicts the administration's own stated principle, while the critics overshoot by minimizing a real Iranian threat and by calling the operation flatly 'illegal' when the war-powers law is genuinely unsettled.

Verified across the spectrum and against primary sources: Trump announced the blockade and the '20% on all cargo shipped' reimbursement on July 13 (quotes read in Al Jazeera, Deseret, USNews, Fortune, Roll Call). The UN's International Maritime Organization stated flatly that 'there is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait,' echoing the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, which was read directly: Article 26 ('No charge may be levied upon foreign ships by reason only of their passage through the territorial sea'), Article 38 (transit passage 'shall not be impeded'), and Article 44 (states bordering straits 'shall not hamper transit passage' and 'there shall be no suspension of transit passage'). The EIA confirms ~20 million barrels/day and ~20% of global petroleum-liquids consumption move through Hormuz. Days before proposing the fee, Secretary of State Rubio had said 'there isn't a nation on Earth that supports having to pay money to go through the straits.'

The right holds real, checkable ground, which is why the needle barely moves. Iran genuinely threatened the chokepoint — claiming to close it and reportedly charging tolls up to $2 million per vessel — so the critics who wave away the Iranian threat overstate. The freedom-of-navigation principle the U.S. invokes to keep the strait open is legally sound (UNCLOS Article 44 bars suspension of transit passage), and the U.S. has used force to keep Hormuz open before (Operation Earnest Will, 1987). Roughly a fifth of the world's oil is at stake. Those facts are the defense's anchor.

Where the defense is weakest is its own central move. The same body of international law that makes Iran's toll illegal also makes the U.S. toll illegal: the IMO said so, UNCLOS says so, and the administration's own Secretary of State said so days earlier. Invoking freedom of navigation to justify a charge that freedom-of-navigation law forbids is a self-contradiction, checkable on the record — and Trump's framing of the toll as a 'beautiful thing,' a 'joint venture' where 'big money will be made,' fits poorly with a security rationale. The critics, in turn, overreach: calling the renewed strikes an 'illegal war' asserts a certainty the War Powers Resolution does not supply (presidents of both parties have stretched the 60-day clock — Obama's lawyers argued Libya wasn't 'hostilities'; Trump's argue the earlier hostilities 'terminated'), and a blockade to keep a strait open is not the mirror image of Iran's closure. Because the critics' factual predicates (there is no legal basis for the toll; it contradicts the U.S.'s own position; the fee is unprecedented) hold up while their 'illegal war' rhetoric overshoots, and the defenders hold a strong Iran-threat and freedom-of-navigation case but stake their justification on a toll the law and their own words reject, the needle lands leans-slightly-left. What the meter cannot decide: whether the U.S. should police global chokepoints at all, whether burden-sharing is fair, whether military force against Iran is wise, and whether transactional 'pay for protection' foreign policy is prudent — those are value questions, not checkable facts.
verdict: On the checkable present-tense facts this leans slightly toward the critics of the Hormuz toll — not because keeping the strait open is wrong (it is legally sound) and not because the president lacks all authority to act, but because the specific move at the center of the day's argument, a mandatory fee to transit an international strait, has no basis in international law and contradicts the administration's own stated position, while the critics overshoot on the war's legality and by minimizing a real Iranian threat. Confirmed across the spectrum and in primary law: on July 13, 2026 Trump announced the U.S. would reinstate an Iran blockade and be 'reimbursed, at the rate of 20% on all cargo shipped' in Hormuz, calling America 'the Guardian of the Hormuz Strait.' The UN's International Maritime Organization said 'there is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait'; UNCLOS Article 26 bars any charge 'by reason only of their passage,' and Article 44 bars suspension of transit passage. Secretary of State Rubio had said days earlier 'there isn't a nation on Earth that supports having to pay money to go through the straits.' The right's anchor facts are also real and keep this from being a rout: Iran genuinely threatened the chokepoint (claiming closure and reportedly charging up to $2 million per vessel), so critics who dismiss the Iran threat overstate; the freedom-of-navigation principle the U.S. invokes is legally correct and the U.S. has kept Hormuz open by force before (Operation Earnest Will, 1987); and ~20% of the world's oil (~20 million barrels/day, per the EIA) is at stake. The needle tips left rather than sitting at zero because of asymmetric overreach: critics who call the renewed strikes a flatly 'illegal war' assert a certainty the War Powers Resolution has never settled (Obama stretched the 60-day clock in Libya; both parties have), and they blur a blockade-to-keep-open with Iran's closure — but the defense stakes its whole justification on a toll that international law, the IMO, and the administration's own Secretary of State all reject, and dresses a 'protection' charge Trump himself called a 'beautiful thing' and 'big money' in the language of freedom of navigation it simultaneously violates. Both overreaches are real; the defenders' cuts a little deeper because the legal predicate for the fee simply is not there and the U.S. condemned the identical Iranian toll days earlier. What the meter does not decide: whether the U.S. should police global chokepoints, whether the fee is fair burden-sharing, whether force against Iran is wise, or whether transactional foreign policy is prudent — those are values, not facts.
verificationNote: The operational facts were anchored to primary sources and cross-spectrum reporting. The international-law core was read directly in the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea via un.org: Article 26 ('No charge may be levied upon foreign ships by reason only of their passage through the territorial sea. Charges may be levied ... as payment only for specific services rendered'), Article 38 (all ships 'enjoy the right of transit passage, which shall not be impeded'), and Article 44 ('States bordering straits shall not hamper transit passage ... There shall be no suspension of transit passage'). The IMO statement ('IMO stands firmly against charging fees for passage through straits used for international navigation. There is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait') was read in the Al Jazeera July 13 report and corroborated in Fortune, Al-Monitor, USNews and CNBC search results. Trump's Truth Social quotes (reinstating the blockade; '20% on all cargo shipped'; 'Guardian of the Hormuz Strait'; 'OPEN ... with or without Iran') were read in Al Jazeera, Deseret and USNews. The Rubio quote ('there isn't a nation on Earth that supports having to pay money to go through the straits') and the Araghchi mockery ('20% is of course too much. We will be fair') were read in Fortune; Deseret (right-leaning) itself flags the contradiction with Rubio's prior position. The oil-volume figure (~20.9 million barrels/day in H1 2025, ~20% of global petroleum-liquids consumption, ~3.5-5.5 million bpd of alternative pipeline capacity) is from the EIA. The June 14 ceasefire (toll-free Hormuz), its July 7 collapse after attacks on commercial ships, and the War Powers posture (strikes began July 7, notice July 10, disputed 60-day reset, Rep. Meeks's symbolic concurrent resolution, a rejected stronger Senate joint resolution) were read in Roll Call and corroborated in Al Jazeera and search results. Iran's toll claim (up to $2 million per vessel) is a party-to-conflict figure marked medium confidence and traced to a March 2026 Fox report on an earlier phase of the crisis. The power-test precedents (Operation Earnest Will, 1987; Obama's 2011 Libya War Powers dispute, with the DOJ OLC opinion and Cato's 'illegal war' critique) and the pattern instances (the April 2025 Ukraine minerals 'payback' fund; the 5% NATO demand; Panama Canal 'military options') were verified in Wikipedia, the OLC memo, Cato, CNN, CFR, CNBC and PIIE. CNN's Hormuz explainer returned HTTP 451, CNBC and Britannica returned 403, and Bloomberg is paywalled, so those points rest on the pages that loaded (Al Jazeera, Deseret, Fortune, Roll Call, Gulf News) plus corroborating search snippets. No claim here rests on an unread page.

left.label: This is a protection racket dressed up as freedom of navigation. Days after the United States condemned Iran for charging ships to cross the Strait of Hormuz — with the Secretary of State insisting no country on Earth should have to pay to transit a strait — the president announced the U.S. would do exactly that, demanding 20% of the value of all cargo passing through. The UN's own maritime agency says flatly there is 'no legal basis' for it, and the law of the sea forbids charging ships for mere passage. You cannot claim to be the guardian of an open waterway while imposing the very toll that closes it to those who won't pay. And this comes wrapped in a renewed blockade and fresh strikes that the president launched without congressional authorization, calling it a 'beautiful thing' and a 'joint venture' where 'big money will be made.' That is not securing global commerce; it is putting a meter on it.
left.framing: The critics' load-bearing claims are checkable and hold: there is no legal basis for a toll to transit an international strait (the IMO said so; UNCLOS Articles 26 and 44 say so), the fee contradicts the administration's own stated position (Rubio, days earlier), and the levy is unprecedented. The honest version also concedes what the meter credits to the other side: Iran did genuinely threaten the chokepoint — claiming to close it and reportedly charging tolls of its own — so the U.S. interest in keeping Hormuz open is real and legally sound (UNCLOS bars suspension of transit passage); and calling the renewed strikes flatly 'illegal' asserts a War Powers conclusion no court has reached and that presidents of both parties have muddied. The strongest form of this case sticks to the toll's illegality and the self-contradiction, not to a blanket 'illegal war' verdict or a claim that the U.S. has no business keeping the strait open.
left.strongestPoint: The toll has no legal basis and the administration said so itself days earlier — a contradiction checkable on the record. The UN's International Maritime Organization stated 'there is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait,' and the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, read directly, bars it: Article 26 ('No charge may be levied upon foreign ships by reason only of their passage through the territorial sea'), Article 44 ('There shall be no suspension of transit passage'). Days before proposing a 20% fee, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said 'there isn't a nation on Earth that supports having to pay money to go through the straits' — the identical principle the U.S. used to condemn Iran's tolls. Invoking freedom of navigation to justify a charge that freedom-of-navigation law forbids, and that your own Secretary of State just rejected, is a self-contradiction, not a policy — and Trump's own framing of it as a 'beautiful thing' and 'big money' undercuts the security rationale.
left.weakestPoint: The camp's strongest facts are wrapped in claims the evidence does not support. Calling the renewed blockade and strikes an 'illegal war' states as settled a War Powers question that is anything but: the president notified Congress under the Resolution, and the 60-day-clock argument he is making (that earlier hostilities 'terminated') is the mirror of the dodge Obama's lawyers used in Libya (that the operation was not 'hostilities') — no court has resolved it. And treating the U.S. blockade as morally identical to Iran's closure blurs a real distinction: keeping a strait open for all traffic is what UNCLOS Article 44 protects, while closing it is what the law forbids. The toll critique is strong and checkable; the 'illegal war' and 'same as Iran' framings run ahead of it and lump a defensible aim (keep Hormuz open) in with an indefensible instrument (charge a toll to do it).
left.unknowns: Whether the renewed strikes are lawful under the War Powers Resolution is genuinely unresolved — the 60-day-reset question turns on contested statutory interpretation no court has settled — so 'illegal war' can be neither confirmed nor refuted in the way the meter demands. Whether the U.S. could actually collect 20% of Hormuz cargo value, whether shippers or flag states would comply, and whether the blockade escalates into a wider war are future contingencies, not present facts.

right.label: Somebody has to keep the Strait of Hormuz open, and it has always been the United States Navy. A fifth of the world's oil moves through that waterway, Iran has been mining it, attacking tankers, and demanding millions per ship to let vessels pass — and the president is not going to let a terror regime hold the global economy hostage. If America is going to spend the blood and treasure to guarantee safe passage for everyone, it is only fair that the countries whose cargo we protect help pay for the protection, rather than free-riding on the U.S. taxpayer as they have for decades. The strait stays open 'with or without Iran.' The Supreme Court and the Constitution give the president the authority to act as commander in chief, and he notified Congress as the law requires. Democrats who cheered every prior use of American power in the Gulf suddenly discover the War Powers Act only when a Republican is in the White House.
right.framing: The defenders' checkable strengths are real: Iran did threaten the chokepoint (claiming closure and reportedly charging tolls of up to $2 million per vessel), ~20% of the world's oil transits Hormuz, keeping the strait open is legally sound (UNCLOS Article 44 bars suspension of transit passage), the U.S. has kept it open by force before (Operation Earnest Will), and the president did notify Congress under the War Powers Resolution. Where the framing breaks down is the central instrument: a mandatory 20% toll to transit an international strait has no basis in the law of the sea (the IMO and UNCLOS say so), and the administration condemned the identical Iranian toll days earlier, so 'we'll be reimbursed for protection' collides with the freedom-of-navigation principle the U.S. is invoking. 'Keep the strait open' is a strong, lawful claim; 'charge everyone 20% to use it' is a rhetorical one the law rejects, and calling a protection charge a 'beautiful thing' and a 'joint venture' undercuts the security story.
right.strongestPoint: The threat and the freedom-of-navigation interest are real and checkable. Roughly a fifth of the world's oil — about 20 million barrels per day, per the EIA — transits Hormuz, and Iran genuinely menaced it: it claimed to close the strait, attacked commercial vessels, and (per Iranian officials) charged transit tolls of up to $2 million per ship. Keeping the strait open is exactly what international law protects: UNCLOS Article 44 says 'there shall be no suspension of transit passage,' so the U.S. position that Hormuz 'will remain OPEN' is legally on solid ground, and the U.S. has enforced that principle before (Operation Earnest Will, 1987-88, reflagging and escorting Kuwaiti tankers). The president also complied with the notification step of the War Powers Resolution, and the critics' flat 'illegal war' verdict asserts a certainty the statute has never supplied.
right.weakestPoint: The defense's affirmative instrument is barred by the very law it invokes. A mandatory 20% fee to transit an international strait has no legal basis — the IMO said 'there is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait,' and UNCLOS Article 26 forbids any charge 'by reason only of ... passage' — and the administration's own Secretary of State said days earlier that no nation should have to pay to cross a strait. You cannot condemn Iran's toll as lawless piracy on Monday and impose your own on Tuesday and call it freedom of navigation. Trump's description of the scheme as a 'beautiful thing,' a 'joint venture' where 'big money will be made,' and the fact that no coastal state has imposed such a toll in the post-1945 era, make the 'this is about security' rationale hard to credit. 'Keep it open' is lawful; 'put a 20% meter on it' is not.
right.unknowns: Whether the toll could ever be enforced or collected — and whether flag states, insurers and shippers would comply rather than reroute or resist — is unknown, as is whether the blockade escalates the conflict. Whether the renewed strikes satisfy the War Powers Resolution's 60-day requirement is unresolved, which cuts against the critics' certainty as much as the defenders'. Iran's closure-and-toll claims are party-to-conflict assertions that could not be independently verified.

facts.claim: On July 13, 2026, President Trump announced the U.S. would reinstate a naval blockade of Iran and be 'reimbursed, at the rate of 20% on all cargo shipped' through the Strait of Hormuz, declaring America 'the Guardian of the Hormuz Strait.'
facts.evidence: Al Jazeera (reading Trump's Truth Social posts): 'We are reinstating the THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE'; 'The U.S.A. will be, from this point forward, known as THE GUARDIAN OF THE HORMUZ STRAIT'; the U.S. would be 'reimbursed, at the rate of 20% on all cargo shipped, for any and all costs necessary to do the job.' USNews/Reuters: 'The Hormuz Strait is OPEN, and will remain OPEN, with or without Iran.'
facts.explanation: The core announcement is common ground and directly quoted across a left-of-center international outlet (Al Jazeera), a wire service (Reuters via USNews), and a right-leaning outlet (Deseret). Confidence is high because the wording is consistent across all three and the White House posted it publicly. The blockade was reported set to begin at 20:00 GMT on Tuesday, July 14.
facts.source: Al Jazeera — Trump says US will become 'guardian' of Strait of Hormuz and collect tolls

corroboration.source: Deseret News (right-leaning) — Trump announces United States will be 'Guardian of the Strait of Hormuz'
US News / Reuters — Trump: Iran Blockade Reinstated, US to Charge 20% on Strait of Hormuz Cargo

facts.claim: There is no legal basis in the international law of the sea for charging a mandatory toll simply to transit an international strait; the UN's International Maritime Organization opposes such fees, and UNCLOS bars charging ships for mere passage.
facts.evidence: IMO statement: 'IMO stands firmly against charging fees for passage through straits used for international navigation. There is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait.' UNCLOS Article 26: 'No charge may be levied upon foreign ships by reason only of their passage through the territorial sea.' Article 44: 'There shall be no suspension of transit passage.'
facts.explanation: This is the load-bearing legal fact, and it cuts both ways. It was read in the primary treaty text (un.org) and in the IMO's own statement (via Al Jazeera and Fortune). It undermines Iran's toll and the U.S. toll equally: charging for passage through an international strait has no basis in UNCLOS, whoever does it. One caveat: Iran has not ratified UNCLOS, and the U.S. has signed but not ratified it, though both treat its navigation provisions as customary international law — which is why the IMO frames the principle as general, not treaty-specific.
facts.source: UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, Articles 26 and 44 (un.org)

corroboration.source: Al Jazeera — IMO 'no legal basis' quote in context
Fortune — IMO opposes the proposal ('no legal basis ... simply to transit a strait')

facts.claim: Days before proposing the 20% fee, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said no nation should have to pay to transit the strait, so the U.S. toll contradicts the administration's own stated position and the freedom-of-navigation principle it invoked against Iran.
facts.evidence: Fortune, quoting Rubio: 'That's international waterway. There isn't a nation on Earth that supports having to pay money to go through the straits.' Deseret (right-leaning) 'highlights the apparent contradiction with Secretary of State Rubio's prior statements that international law prohibits charging tolls on international waterways.'
facts.explanation: The contradiction is checkable and is conceded by a right-leaning outlet (Deseret), which is the strongest form of corroboration. The administration condemned Iranian tolls on the same freedom-of-navigation principle it is now overriding with its own toll. This is placed on the 'left' because it is the critics' central point, but it is a factual contradiction on the record, not merely a rhetorical charge. Caveat: the administration could argue its fee is a 'reimbursement for security services' rather than a toll for 'mere passage,' but the IMO's and UNCLOS's language ('specific services rendered,' non-discrimination) does not obviously reach a blanket 20%-of-all-cargo levy.
facts.source: Fortune — Rubio quote and Iran's mocking response

corroboration.source: Deseret News (right-leaning) — notes contradiction with Rubio's prior statements

facts.claim: Roughly 20% of the world's oil — about 20 million barrels per day — transits the Strait of Hormuz, making it the world's most important oil chokepoint.
facts.evidence: U.S. Energy Information Administration: in the first half of 2025, oil flows through the strait averaged 'about 20.9 million barrels per day,' equal to 'about 20% of global petroleum liquids consumption' and one-quarter of total global maritime-traded oil, with an estimated 3.5-5.5 million bpd of alternative pipeline capacity via Saudi Arabia and the UAE.
facts.explanation: This is the defense's strongest structural fact and is not in dispute; it is drawn from the U.S. government's own energy statistics agency and corroborated across reporting. It establishes the genuine global stake in keeping the strait open — which is why the critics who minimize the Iran threat overshoot. It does not, however, establish that a toll is lawful; it establishes only that the chokepoint matters.
facts.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration — World Oil Transit Chokepoints / Strait of Hormuz

corroboration.source: Gulf News — strait carries 'roughly a fifth of the world's oil supply'

facts.claim: Iran threatened the chokepoint before the U.S. move — claiming to close the strait and, per Iranian officials, reportedly charging transit tolls of up to $2 million per vessel.
facts.evidence: Fox News (March 22, 2026): Iranian lawmaker Alaeddin Boroujerdi said 'collecting $2 million as transit fees from some vessels crossing the strait reflects Iran's strength'; Windward AI reported Strait traffic 'near collapse.' Reuters/USNews and CNBC note the July move followed Iran's claim to have 'closed' the strait after attacks on commercial vessels.
facts.explanation: Graded 'mixed' and placed on the 'right' because it favors the defense but rests on party-to-conflict claims. That Iran menaced the strait, attacked ships and demanded tolls is well attested; the specific $2 million figure comes from an Iranian lawmaker (a party to the dispute) and a March 2026 report on an earlier phase, and whether Iran actually 'closed' the strait versus disrupted it is contested. Confidence is medium: the threat is real, the precise figures and the extent of closure are not independently verified. The Iranian toll being illegal under the same UNCLOS principle is the mirror of the U.S. toll being illegal.
facts.source: Fox News — Iran chokes Strait of Hormuz with reported $2M tanker toll

corroboration.source: US News / Reuters — June ceasefire prohibited Iran from imposing charges; ceasefire 'over'

facts.claim: A June 14, 2026 U.S.-Iran ceasefire had provided toll-free Hormuz passage and barred Iran from charging fees; it collapsed after Iranian attacks on commercial ships around July 6-7, and Trump declared the truce 'over.'
facts.evidence: USNews/Reuters: 'A temporary ceasefire deal the U.S. and Iran signed in mid-June explicitly prohibited Tehran from imposing any charges on commercial ships passing through the strait,' and 'that deal has been so undermined ... that Trump last week declared the ceasefire was over.' Roll Call: a June memorandum 'intended to shore up peace indefinitely ... collapsed after Iranian attacks on commercial shipping last week'; Trump declared the truce over July 7.
facts.explanation: The ceasefire, its toll-free terms and its collapse are corroborated across Reuters (USNews) and Roll Call and an Al Jazeera June 14 report. Confidence is medium because sources describe overlapping April and June understandings and differ slightly on framing (a spring ceasefire followed by a June 14 memorandum); the toll-free Hormuz provision and the early-July collapse are consistent across them. This underlies the 'reversal' framing: the U.S. that barred Iran's fee in June is now imposing its own.
facts.source: US News / Reuters — Trump: Iran Blockade Reinstated, US to Charge 20% on Strait of Hormuz Cargo

corroboration.source: Roll Call — June memorandum collapsed after attacks on commercial shipping
Al Jazeera — US-Iran ceasefire deal announced; Strait of Hormuz reopening (June 14, 2026)

facts.claim: The renewed strikes and blockade were undertaken by executive action; Trump notified Congress under the War Powers Resolution (strikes began July 7, notice July 10), and Democrats dispute both the war's legality and whether the 60-day clock can reset.
facts.evidence: Roll Call: Trump 'formally notified Congress on July 10 of resumed military strikes against Iran,' calling the July 7 operations 'defensive' and 'limited' under his 'constitutional authority as commander in chief'; after the earlier ceasefire he had told Congress hostilities 'terminated,' arguing the 60-day deadline no longer applied; Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.) sponsored a 'largely symbolic' concurrent resolution to end the war, and the Senate rejected a stronger joint resolution.
facts.explanation: The War Powers posture is corroborated in Roll Call and search reporting. Graded 'both' because the notification (a point for the defense) and the disputed 60-day reset (a point for the critics) are both established facts. Confidence is medium: the legal question — whether declaring earlier hostilities 'terminated' resets the clock — is genuinely unsettled and mirrors the Obama-era Libya dispute. This is context on the war's legality, not a determinative fact; no court has ruled the operation unlawful.
facts.source: Roll Call — Trump informs Congress of renewed military action against Iran

corroboration.source: The Hill — Trump formally informs Congress of Iran war resumption

facts.claim: Charging 20% of the value of all cargo transiting Hormuz would be an unprecedented levy of dubious practicality; no coastal state has imposed a mandatory toll on a natural strait in the post-1945 era.
facts.evidence: Gulf News: the arrangement 'would be unprecedented in the post-1945 era — no coastal state has imposed mandatory tolls on natural straits,' and could set a precedent for other chokepoints. CNN Business's explainer was headlined 'Trump offers US protection in the Strait of Hormuz for a 20% fee. How would that work?' — framing collection as an open practical question.
facts.explanation: Graded 'mostly-supported' and placed on the 'left' to hold the critics' feasibility argument to the same standard. The 'unprecedented' claim is well attested (Gulf News); the practical-collection problem (who assesses 20% of what value, and how it is enforced against non-compliant flag states and insurers) is real but speculative, so it is context rather than a settled fact. CNN's page returned HTTP 451 on direct fetch, so this rests on Gulf News plus the CNN headline. It illustrates that even setting law aside, the mechanism is untested.
facts.source: Gulf News — New Hormuz toll fee a 'beautiful thing', says Trump

corroboration.source: CNN Business — Trump offers US protection in the Strait of Hormuz for a 20% fee. How would that work? (HTTP 451 on fetch; headline)

powerLens.thesis: The power test here runs along two axes that have both role-reversed in living memory. First, unauthorized executive war-making: in 2026 a Republican president resumes strikes on Iran and argues the War Powers Resolution's 60-day clock does not bind him because earlier hostilities 'terminated'; Democrats call it lawless. But in 2011 a Democratic president, Barack Obama, kept bombing Libya past the 60-day deadline by arguing the operation did not amount to 'hostilities' at all — a dodge the libertarian Cato Institute called 'President Obama's Illegal War' and that many Republicans denounced, while Democrats defended it. Each party discovers the War Powers Resolution precisely when the other party holds the trigger. Second, keeping Hormuz open by force is bipartisan American practice — Reagan's Operation Earnest Will reflagged and escorted Kuwaiti tankers through the Gulf in 1987-88 over congressional objection — so the novelty in 2026 is not the Navy in the strait but the toll booth: no prior administration of either party charged the world to use it. The durable, above-the-fray question is whether the U.S. role as guarantor of freedom of navigation is a public good it provides or a service it now bills for — and that question will outlast this president. The needle, though, rests on the present record: the toll has no legal basis, it contradicts the administration's own stated position, ~20% of world oil is at stake, Iran did threaten the strait, and the war's legality is genuinely unsettled.

precedent.case: Obama's 2011 Libya intervention (War Powers Resolution 60-day dispute) and Reagan's 1987-88 Operation Earnest Will in the Strait of Hormuz.
precedent.what: In 2011, President Obama continued U.S. military operations over Libya past the War Powers Resolution's 60-day limit, with the DOJ Office of Legal Counsel and State Department arguing the U.S. role did not constitute 'hostilities' requiring congressional authorization; the House rebuked him and rejected an authorization, and critics across the spectrum (including Cato) called the war illegal. In 1987-88, President Reagan launched Operation Earnest Will — reflagging Kuwaiti oil tankers under the U.S. flag and escorting them through the Gulf to keep the Strait of Hormuz open against Iranian mines and attacks — the largest naval convoy operation since World War II, undertaken over congressional opposition to the reflagging but without charging any state for the protection.
precedent.mirror: Libya maps onto today's War Powers fight: both presidents kept a Middle East military operation going past or around the 60-day clock by redefining the conflict (Obama: not 'hostilities'; Trump: hostilities 'terminated' and restarted), and the party in power defended the maneuver each time. Earnest Will maps onto the Hormuz mission itself: the U.S. has used force to keep the strait open before, which validates the 'keep it open' aim — but Reagan never billed the world 20% for it, which isolates exactly what is new and legally unsupported in 2026: the toll.
precedent.source: Cato Institute (libertarian) — President Obama's Illegal War

corroboration.source: U.S. DOJ Office of Legal Counsel — Authority to Use Military Force in Libya (April 1, 2011)
Wikipedia — Operation Earnest Will (Reagan reflagging and escorting Kuwaiti tankers through the Gulf, 1987-88)

flips.then: In 2011, Democrats largely defended President Obama's decision to continue the Libya air war past the War Powers Resolution's 60-day deadline, accepting the administration's argument that the operation did not amount to 'hostilities' and therefore needed no congressional authorization.
flips.now: In 2026, Democrats denounce Trump's resumed Iran strikes as an 'illegal war' and insist the 60-day clock binds him and cannot be reset — treating the War Powers Resolution as an iron limit when a Republican is commander in chief. The reverence for the clock is calibrated to who is in the White House.
flips.source: Lawfare — Libya, the War Powers Resolution, and whether reversion to a support role counts as 'terminating' hostilities

flips.then: When President Obama bypassed the 60-day clock in Libya in 2011, many Republicans objected that he was flouting the War Powers Resolution and the Constitution's allocation of war powers to Congress, and some (with libertarians) called the intervention illegal and unauthorized.
flips.now: In 2026, the right defends Trump's resumed Iran campaign as a lawful exercise of the commander-in-chief power and waves off the War Powers objections as partisan — the same objections it raised against Obama. The worry about unauthorized presidential war has evaporated now that it is their president waging it, and the transactional toll is embraced as 'fairness' rather than scrutinized as the novel, legally unsupported step it is.
flips.source: Cato Institute (libertarian) — President Obama's Illegal War (documenting the right/libertarian objection to Obama-era unilateralism)

powerLens.longRun: Strip out which party holds the White House and two through-lines are constant. On war powers: the president in office treats the 60-day clock as a formality to be redefined around, his party supplies the legal theory, and the out-party rediscovers Congress's prerogatives — Obama said Libya wasn't 'hostilities'; Trump says the Iran hostilities 'terminated' and restarted. On the strait: the U.S. Navy keeps Hormuz open by force under presidents of both parties (Reagan did it in 1987), because freedom of navigation genuinely serves the U.S. and the world. What is new in 2026, and what no prior administration of either party did, is to put a price on the passage — to convert the public good of an open strait into a 20% toll. The prediction when power changes hands: the next president will inherit both the expanded war-making latitude and, if it stands, the precedent that the guarantor of a chokepoint may bill the world to use it. What survives every flip is the present-tense record — the toll has no legal basis, it contradicts the U.S.'s own stated position, the war's legality is unsettled, and Iran really did threaten the strait.
powerLens.appliedToScore: The power test docks reasoning-honesty from both camps for advantage-driven inconsistency on war powers — Democrats' insistence that the 60-day clock is inviolable is the reverse of their posture on Obama's Libya, and the right's dismissal of War Powers objections reverses its Obama-era complaints (the libertarian/constitutional-conservative strand that objected to both is the more consistent one). That two-sided opportunism is one reason neither reasoning score is high. But consistency is not the needle. On the present checkable facts — is there a legal basis for the toll (no), does it contradict the administration's own stated position (yes), is ~20% of world oil at stake (yes), did Iran threaten the strait (yes), and is the war's legality settled (no) — the critics have the better-documented case on the toll's illegality and the self-contradiction while overreaching on 'illegal war,' and the defenders have a strong Iran-threat and freedom-of-navigation case while resting their justification on a toll the law and their own words reject. That asymmetry, modest but real, is why the needle lands leans-slightly-left rather than at zero.
powerLens.unknowns: The power test measures consistency of principle, not who is right on today's facts. Documenting that both parties flip on war powers does not make this week's toll lawful or unlawful, nor resolve whether the renewed strikes satisfy the War Powers Resolution — a genuinely unsettled question the meter cannot decide.

pattern.thesis: This is not an isolated Iran-crisis decision; it is the latest instance of a transactional foreign-policy pattern in which the administration reframes a U.S. security guarantee or public good as a fee, share, or payment the U.S. collects — and treating the Hormuz toll in isolation keeps the argument off that trend. The recurring move is to tell allies and users of U.S.-provided security that they must pay up: a share of Ukraine's minerals in exchange for wartime aid, a 5% GDP demand on NATO members framed as ending their 'free-riding,' pressure to reclaim leverage over the Panama Canal, and now a 20% toll on Hormuz cargo cast as 'reimbursement for protection.' The recurring question is whether this is legitimate burden-sharing that ends decades of allied free-riding, or a protection-racket logic that converts the rules-based order the U.S. built into a set of tollbooths — and whether each instance is separately defensible or part of one posture.

campaigns.where: Strait of Hormuz toll (today's issue), July 2026
campaigns.what: Trump announced a 20% fee on all cargo transiting Hormuz as 'reimbursement' for U.S. protection, alongside a reinstated Iran blockade, declaring the U.S. 'the Guardian of the Hormuz Strait.' The IMO and UNCLOS provide no legal basis for a toll to transit an international strait, and the administration condemned Iran's identical toll days earlier.
campaigns.authorization: No legal basis in the law of the sea; contradicts the administration's own prior position; enforcement untested
campaigns.source: Al Jazeera — Trump says US will become 'guardian' of Strait of Hormuz and collect tolls

corroboration.source: Fortune — IMO 'no legal basis'; Rubio's contradictory prior statement

campaigns.what: The U.S. and Ukraine signed a Reconstruction Investment Fund giving the U.S. a stake in Ukraine's future mineral, oil and gas revenues; Trump repeatedly framed it as Ukraine 'paying back' the U.S. for military aid, having initially demanded a $500 billion share of Ukraine's rare earths in exchange for support already provided.
campaigns.authorization: Signed deal; the 'reimbursement for aid' framing dropped in the final text
campaigns.source: CNN — Here's what's in Trump's Ukraine minerals deal and how it affects the war

corroboration.source: Council on Foreign Relations — What's the Deal with Trump's Ukraine Mineral Agreement?

campaigns.where: NATO '5% or we may not defend you' demand, 2025
campaigns.what: Trump demanded NATO members raise defense spending to 5% of GDP ('They can all afford it, but they should be at 5%, not 2%') and cast doubt on whether the U.S. would defend allies that spend too little, framing the alliance as free-riding on the U.S. taxpayer — the same 'pay for protection' logic applied to Hormuz cargo.
campaigns.authorization: Political pressure; most allies moved toward the 5% target by mid-2025
campaigns.source: CNBC — Can Trump get NATO allies to spend up to 5% of GDP on defense?

corroboration.source: PIIE — Trump's Five Percent Doctrine and NATO Defense Spending

pattern.takeaway: The recurring question is whether reframing U.S. security guarantees as fees the U.S. collects — a share of Ukraine's minerals, 5% from NATO, a 20% Hormuz toll — is overdue burden-sharing or a protection-racket logic that erodes the freedom-of-navigation and alliance commitments the U.S. built and benefits from. The 'one-off' tell to watch: anyone treating the Hormuz toll purely as a routine security-cost recovery (the right) or purely as gangsterism (the left) without situating it in the string of 'make them pay' moves that preceded it. The facts that don't move are that the toll has no legal basis, ~20% of world oil transits Hormuz, Iran did threaten the strait, and the administration condemned the identical Iranian toll days earlier.
pattern.unknowns: The pattern is context that reframes the stakes, not a thumb on the needle, which rests on the present issue's checkable facts. Situating the Hormuz toll among the Ukraine and NATO demands does not by itself make it lawful or unlawful, nor prove a single coordinated doctrine rather than a series of separate decisions — only that it is not sensibly judged in isolation.

notAdjudicable: Whether the United States should act as the guarantor of freedom of navigation in global chokepoints at all, or whether that role is imperial overreach, is a values question about America's place in the world the meter cannot decide.

Whether it is fair for the countries whose cargo benefits from U.S. protection to help pay for it — burden-sharing versus free-riding — is a values judgment about distributive fairness, not a checkable fact.

Whether renewed military force against Iran is wise or reckless — its effect on escalation, oil prices, and the risk of a wider war — is a judgment about consequences this week's events cannot settle.

Whether a transactional, 'pay for protection' foreign policy strengthens U.S. interests or corrodes the alliances and legal order the U.S. built is a genuine strategic-values dispute distinct from the checkable facts about the toll.
Whether the president's motive is to secure global commerce or to extract revenue and leverage is a question about intent that the public record cannot definitively establish.
biasWatch: The dominant distortion here is letting the flag decide the law — treating 'is charging to cross a strait legal and principled?' as a question whose answer depends on whether the United States or Iran is doing the charging. Critics collapse two separate questions — 'is there a legal basis for a mandatory toll to transit an international strait, and does the U.S. fee contradict its own stated position?' (checkable: no basis; yes, it contradicts Rubio and the IMO) and 'is the renewed blockade therefore an illegal war?' (a War Powers conclusion no court has reached and that both parties have muddied) — into a single guilty verdict, and they blur a blockade-to-keep-the-strait-open with Iran's closure, which international law treats very differently. Defenders collapse the mirror image — 'a fifth of the world's oil is at stake and we keep the strait open, so charging 20% is just fairness' — using a real security interest and a lawful aim (keep Hormuz open) to launder an unlawful instrument (a toll UNCLOS and the IMO forbid, and the administration itself rejected days earlier), while dismissing the contradiction with Rubio and the 'beautiful thing / big money' framing that undercuts the security story. The tell to watch in yourself and in commentary: notice whether a writer separates the empirical questions (did Trump announce a 20% toll and a blockade; does international law permit tolls for transit; did the U.S. condemn Iran's identical toll days earlier; how much oil transits Hormuz — all answerable) from the value-and-legal questions (whether the U.S. should police chokepoints, whether the fee is fair, whether the war is wise, whether the strikes satisfy the War Powers Resolution — not answerable by this week's evidence), or whether they smuggle a confident answer to one to settle the other. Then run the swap test: would today's Republicans accept a Democratic president bypassing the 60-day War Powers clock and billing the world to cross a strait (Obama stretched that clock in Libya, and many on the right objected), and would today's Democrats accept the identical toll and strikes under a president they trusted (many defended Obama's Libya dodge)? If your view of whether this is guardianship or gangsterism flips with the party in power, you are scoring the team, not the principle — but keep the asymmetry the swap test cannot erase: the toll has no legal basis, it contradicts the administration's own words, ~20% of world oil is at stake, Iran did threaten the strait, and the war's legality is genuinely unsettled, all regardless of who is president.
